turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- from the statute, the definitional provisions of which were identical to those voided in the Baggett and Dombrowski cases are unconstitutionally fraud, vague and indefinite. The facts in this case are comparatively simple. It's a direct appeal from a final order and judgment of a statutory three-judge court sitting in Maryland, dismissing the appellant's complaint on the grounds of a prior precedent to this Court. The appellant, a Quaker, is an author of works of fiction and nonfiction, and a teacher of Creative Writing at the Johns Hopkins University. He was invited in the summer of 1966 to teach Creative Writing at the University of Maryland for the academic year of 1966-1967. He was to be paid $7,000 over 10 months. In July, he received by mail a copy of the contract to be signed and certain other documents including the certification of applicant for public employment which if I may I'd like to read to the Court. It's captioned required by law, Article 85A, paragraph 13 Annotated Code of Maryland 1957. It appears by the way in the transcript at page 11 in the appendix of the appellant's brief at page 16A. It states, “I, -- leaving space for the name --, do hereby certify that I am not engaged in one way or another in an attempt to overthrow the government of the United States, or the State of Maryland, or any political subdivision of either of them, by force or violence. I further certify that I understand the aforegoing statement is made subject to the penalties of perjury prescribed in Article 27, Section 439 of the Annotated Code of Maryland,” -- and there's a space for the date and the signature.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: The appellant signed the contract, executed the other papers with the exception of the loyalty of. He returned these documents and advised his correspondent in a separate letter that on conscientious grounds he would not sign the required oath. He offered to teach by the way without compensation pending adjudication of the constitutional issues. He received a reply informing him that the university could not accept his services until he had executed and filed the oath. On August 19, 1966, he filed a class action.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: I beg your pardon sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Do you think this oath is more or less different from the oath that the (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: More or less specific?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: I would say in the context to the statute which is the only way that the oath can be read, it's much less specific.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Oh, what I am trying to (Inaudible) from the statute.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, it's the appellant's --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Is this the only oath that he was required to sign?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: At this time, yes Justice Harlan.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: However, he has to take this oath as authorized under a specific statute and it's our contention that the Gerende case, if that's what you're referring to Justice Harlan, does not really hold for the proposition that the oath can be read without the statute. And if it does, the subsequent decisions of this Court will make it quite clear that an oath cannot be considered outside of the context of its statute. And --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And it sort of incorporated all of the statutes in this (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: We believe that that's what the Elfbrandt case indicates.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I know, but what about the oath that was sworn?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: The oath itself is quite --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The oath in reference to the rest of the statute is (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: It has two references. It has a caption and it also provides that it's subject to the penalties of perjury and the only way in which this could be subject to the penalties of perjuries on a statutory basis which is also a reference to the statute ultimately.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Penalty of perjury in the different statutes --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes, but this would be statutory perjury and the only statute it provides for the oath happens to be the overact itself. In addition, the phrase in one way or another we contend is unduly broad and vague. But Your Honor --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But suppose this oath stood alone. Suppose the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: If it stood alone --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- if we would go along with respect to its relationship to the statute, the implications in that relationship, suppose the teachers just declined to take this particular oath.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: And that's all the statute said Justice Fortas.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: That's all?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well this particular oath to me would still suggest is unduly vague to the extent that it has the phrase in one way or another in it which defies the definition --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Yes, but it also got the phrase, overthrow the government by force or violence.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And it didn't relate to overthrowing the Government in any other way, does it?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, but it says in one way or another. That could --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: By force or violence.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: By force or violence. That could be read to mean the “in one way or another” phrase that mere adherence to an organization that's engaged in an attempt or about to be engaged in an attempt to overthrow the government by force --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But of course, hasn't been so read?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: It -- it's not clear. The only judicial determination of the statute and oath other than the Gerende case is the Court of Appeals of Maryland's decision in the Shub case in 1950. And while -- one could say that the Court of Appeals of Maryland attempted to narrow the construction of the actual statute, it certainly isn't clear and it's our position that the Court of Appeals reading of the statute would contemplate mere advocacy of abstract ideas within the ambit of the statute and therefore the oath. And also mere membership, mere knowing membership in an organization, not even necessarily qualified to active membership and certainly not qualified to membership with specific intent to support the goals, the unlawful goals of the organization.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well is that your argument that the oath as construed by the highest court of the state, it means something more than it seems to say?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, our argument is that the oath as read with the statute as construed by the highest court of the state clearly contemplates that which this Court declared unconstitutionally broad and vague in Keyishian and Elfbrandt and Baggett.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But I believe the statute out as it -- would you perhaps -- what is your presentation to us?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: If the statute is just left out entirely, well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- we have great deal of difficulty leaving the statute out. There's no other authority --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- in the oath.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: But leaving the statute out, it's our position that the phrase “in one way or another" which is a phrase admitted -- accepted by the Court of Appeals of Maryland and by this Court in the Gerende case has no definite meaning. And if it does have a definite meaning, it's too broad.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: So it come back to reliance on that phrase for all of them on the construction of the act on the Shub case.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, if you insist Justice Fortas that we have to leave the statute out, I --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I wasn't trying to insist that; I was trying to --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: I do rely upon that phrase but we also make an --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: (Inaudible) relied on your (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: We also make another substantive argument with respect to the oath if it's read by itself and that is we believe that these negative disclaimers by themselves, by their very nature, violations of the First Amendment that -- just is across the board requirement that one say, “I am not disloyal”, whatever that means. But that's a violation of the First Amendment. In addition, we suggest as the brief amicus curiae of the AAUP did that this kind of an oath, not giving anybody an opportunity to explain or get clarification of the oath or explain his reasons for not stating he is not disloyal that by not giving any opportunity for a hearing or an explanation that the oath violates procedural due process in the First Amendment context.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Would you say that if you required a teacher to swear that if he would uphold the constitution of the United States that would be unconstitutional.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's a harder case Justice Fortas of course. I think we might be willing to argue that there's no warrant for an across the board declaration by all persons but I think we might fall back to an intermediate position and suggest that if teachers were singled out as the only group to swear, only group among public employees that that would raise people protection problems for us if --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, all federal employees have to take an oath to have effect?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Are you suggesting that that would be unconstitutional for federal employees or in the group of federal employees?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: No. In this case, we don't think we have to reach the question whether an affirmative declaration of allegiance is unconstitutional. We certainly see that there's a basis in the text of the constitution itself for this kind of a declaration. So I can't answer that question so far as my client is concerned. Definitively, we're faced with a negative disclaimer and it's not an affirmative declaration.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: We'd have a lot more trouble, I must admit with that affirming --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: You're not going to affirm (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, we do go so far as to suggest that a loyalty oath that requires a negative disclaimer, I am not disloyal, I am not a subversive person, I am not engaged in, that this carries such encored connotations that it is itself an undue burden on the First Amendment. So far as an affirmative statement of allegiance is concerned, we don't go so far in this case as to argue that that would be unconstitutional. We'd like to see that case before we had to address ourselves to the particular issue of course. If I may proceed --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What do you argue on one -- in one way or another the theory that one way or another (Inaudible)?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: It could be taken to mean other than by force or violence and certainly even if read to be modified by force or violence, it could be taken to mean adherence to an organization engaged or about to be engaged and overthrow without any specific --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: In Maryland (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, that's not quite accurate Justice Harlan. Maryland didn't do that and Attorney General of Maryland made a recommendation. Just as an Attorney General in Gerende stated that he would advise various officials in the state to give the oath that was approved or apparently approved in Gerende. As a matter of fact Justice Harlan, that oath was never consistently given, there are subdivisions in this -- in the State of Maryland they're still giving the oath in terms of the definitional phrase in Section 1 of the Act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So far as this Act is concerned (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Do you not?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's right. So far as the oath that Mr. Whitehill was requested or required to sign, it did not include the membership provision. I will argue further along that the Attorney General probably has no authority or not adequate authority, or power to rewrite the oath and you certainly has adequate authority or power to rewrite the statute which was judicially construed albeit not as clearly as we would like in the Shub case. Basically, appellant agrees that the first issue before the Court is the current status of the Gerende decision. However, before addressing or readdressing myself as the case maybe to Gerende, it might be appropriate for me to just outline briefly the four substantive or meritorious grounds on which appellant contends that the oath and the statute are unconstitutional. First, I suggest that it's our position that the oath and the statute were unconstitutionally broad, vague and uncertain in degradation of the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause. Second, we contend that this indiscriminate loyalty of or non-disloyalty disclaimer, otherwise unconstitutionally infringes the First and Fourteenth Amendment freedoms of belief, association and academic freedom. Third, in violation of procedural due process, the oath and the statute further infringe the First and Fourteenth Amendment interests by denying public employment without affording any hearing on questions of loyalty or subversiveness and by shifting burdens of proof with respect to these questions to the applicant. And fourth, together the statute and the oath constitute an unlawful bill of attainder. Now this Court's decision in Gerende, the 1951 very brief per curiam decision in Gerende involved Section 15 of the Act, so-called Candidate's Oath. In Gerende, the Court relied upon the Court of Appeals of Maryland's construction of that provision in Shub versus Simpson. Perhaps if I could refresh Your Honor's memories with respect to Gerende, in that case you said, “The scope of the state law was passed on -- in Shub". We read this decision to hold that to obtain a place on a Maryland ballot for candidate need only make oath that he is not a person who is engaged "in one way or another in the attempt to overthrow the government by force or violation,” and that he is not knowingly a member of an organization engaged in such an attempt. At the bar of this Court, the Attorney General of the State of Maryland declared that he would advise the proper authorities to and accept an affidavit in these terms to satisfying in full the statutory requirement. Under these circumstances and with this understanding, the judgment of the Court of Appeals is affirmed. Now appellees insist that the decision in Gerende in effect severs the certification or oath from the statute and insulates it, claimed innocuous on its face as we've just discussed from constitutional attack. Appellant respectfully disagrees. First, the certification is not innocuous on its phase. Both the Supreme --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What happened if Professor Whitehill actually (Inaudible)?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Professor Whitehill is still teaching at the Johns Hopkins University, he recently published a novel as a matter of fact. But he lives in Maryland. He was ready, willing and able to fulfill the contract at any time during his term. He is currently a lecturer in English, teaching Creative Writing at Johns Hopkins.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: The University of Maryland, the College of Art.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: How long was he a part of the --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: For the academic year 1966-1967, an academic year which would have ended in June of 1967th.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: I don't believe so Your Honor. No more than there was in Bond versus Floyd last term. Professor Whitehill would have claimed on back wages at very least.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: How much is his (Voice Overlap) --?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: I have no idea.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well this would have been an addition to his job at Hopkins. There is no real question of mitigation. This was a one-day or two-day a week operation and it's not the sort of thing that a man could go out and mitigate. He was filling in for a specific post, you don't have too many instructors of Creative Writing around nor do you in -- in a given state have too many opportunities to teach Creative Writing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The question of mootness (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: The question of mootness has not been raised and as I suggested, the Bond case indicates that it would not appropriately be raised and there's Johnson versus Branch, the Fourth Circuit decision dealing with dismissal of certain school teachers. It indicate that the damages themselves would be adequate -- of course further there -- this is a continuing situation maybe not directly with respect to Professor Whitehill. But we have a file of additional people and could be -- we could come in to court tomorrow and file a new suit in the District of Maryland and if need to be there, or continually cases coming up in Maryland at this time. Now as we suggested, the certification is not innocuous on its face. First, the Supreme Court and the Maryland Court of Appeals authorized a prohibition of knowing membership in an organization engaged in an attempt to violently overthrow the government --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Rosen, suppose you struck one way or another --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well if you struck in one way or another Justice Marshall, I supposed that the oath would become comparatively unvague. However, we would still rely upon our two or three other arguments that first this kind of a negative oath, a negative disclaimer itself violates the First Amendment. It's taken up on point too in our brief in effect. And second, that by not affording any procedural --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You mean, you can't ask somebody welcome after he intend to violate the laws of the United States?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That might be what we've been forced to ask Justice Marshall. We -- we're not saying that you can't in all circumstances certainly ask somebody whether he will adhere to the laws of the United States. The implications of asking the negative as Dr. Glass -- eventually Glass suggested, it's like asking a man whether he's being unfaithful. The implication carries --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Would you mind taking the oath when you became a member of the bar?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Did I mind taking the oath --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- when I became a member of the bar? It was an affirmative oath as I recall. I might say, I didn't mind taking the oath for my employment with the University of Maryland. I found that it exceedingly offensive.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: No, I was talking about the bar.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: No, I did not mind taking the oath which as I recall, in Connecticut it was an oath or affirmation to support the laws and constitution.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I think, would it be any different to -- do you ask the question (Inaudible)?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's true. And of course, the recent decisions of this Court in the Konigsberg and staff locations indicated that this kind of inquiry might be appropriate.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Do you think (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well we have suggest -- we have suggested in our oath that -- I mean, in our brief that the -- that particularly with respect to the question of subversiveness which --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's a different area. It doesn't involve the First --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: I supposed one can be asked that it's germane to whether or not you're -- you should be admitted to practice.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Even if it's a negative oath.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: It's just a negative oath, Justice White. It's not in the First Amendment area. What's you're asking Mr. Whitehill is to say is I am not subversive. That is, I do not hold or act upon certain political ideals which is purely First Amendment matter. If you ask a man who is to be admitted to the practice of law, have you ever been convicted of a felony? That doesn't to me raise very many First Amendment implications. We're in the area of the First Amendment and this as the Court will notice is a very special area and distinguishable at least on that basis.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: This is all raised in the present tense to with me. I am not indeed -- he's got to say --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- I'm not indeed in an attempt to overthrow the government of the United States by force and violence.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well that raises precisely --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- one of the grounds --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- that this is quite immaterial direct test but it's rather a resilient thing to require a grown man to say as (Inaudible). But that's not our problem. We asked him to say whether -- as of this moment of time, is he engaged in attempting to overthrow --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- the Government of the United States by force and violence? Well, that's very different from asking are you engaged or have you engaged or do you intend to engage in subversive activities.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well that's what the oath says and I agree with Justice Fortas, it is rather silly.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But you also have said that he is being required to say that he is -- won't engage in a subversive activity --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- that's not this one.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, we say of course that the phrase “in one way or another” means subversive activities of course --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But all of that is modified, there's a comma and it's modified by the phrase by the course of violence.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, the Court --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I suppose you can -- the idea of the question as this with literary product if it overthrow the government by poison, by dum-dum bullets, by machine guns, by --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- possession of whisky.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Of course we don't read that phrase as you do. At this moment Justice Fortas, further, we suggest that the very narrowness of the oath if that's the way you read it which speaks and as you suggested foolishly of, “Are you at present, this moment, this instant in history, engaged in an attempt to overthrow the government?" The very narrowness of that inquiry only highlights Section 14 of the statute and Section 10 of the statute. Section 10 says that no subversive person shall be employed by the state. Section 14 provides for continual surveillance of persons in the employee of the state, including teachers, including Mr. Whitehill had he been employed by the state. Continual surveillance of these people to determine whether --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Oh, I don't see (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- in the future they become subversive persons.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I don't see that those are before us.Are you arguing that they're before us (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes we are. We are arguing -- our basic argument Justice Fortas is that you cannot in the light of the Elfbrandt decision in particular read this oath without its statute. In Elfbrandt, you'll recall Justice Fortas that the oath on its face was apparently innocuous. It was even an affirmative declaration of allegiance. One didn't find out what the defect in the oath was until he read the statute and discovered the legislature had put a specific gloss upon the oath and a gloss that this Court found was unduly broad. The same thing is true in this case. We can't conceive of how the oath can be read without the statute, where the Attorney General himself, in his brief suggests that the authority for requiring the appellant to execute the certification is found in Section 11 of the statute. That's the only authority that the Attorney General that any state agent has for giving this oath. As Justice Sobeloff suggested in his special concurrence in Whitehill, I am inclined to agree that the prescribed oath or statement is not to be judged in isolation but in conjunction with the statute. For in the absence of a statute, there would be no authority for demanding any statement under the penalty of perjury. There is no other basis for this statement to be requested or rather demanded of these people. That's the only authority the state has to ask the -- it's not possible, we submit to therefore read the oath without its underlying statutory authority.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, that maybe true but the highest judicial -- not that the -- the highest law enforcement officer of your state as I understand it has said that, "Yes, that's true but it's not for you or for us to read the statute". And it's not that we -- what we think it might mean, he has told us that it means and requires no more than this oath --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: We submit --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- is that true?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- that he has no authority to say that.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well that's not for us to decide, is it?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Certainly it is.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Just as in --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: A matter of Maryland law.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Just as in the Sweezy case, this Court decided that it could not really tell whether the Attorney General had authority to ask the questions he was asking because he --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, that's a question of Maryland law, isn't it?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Whether he has --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Whether he has authority --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- authority --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- to do that. That's certainly not a matter of federal law.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: It certainly is in the First Amendment area, that's the Sweezy case indicates.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: On the Sweezy case Justice Stewart, the Attorney General was authorized somehow, by the legislature of New Hampshire to make inquiry to investigate subversive activities in the State of New Hampshire. The Supreme Court, this Court overturned I believe a contempt citation of one called Sweezy basically on the grounds that it didn't know what the authority, the scope of authority of the Attorney General was in the -- in this First Amendment area without some clear -- very clear statement to the authority of the Attorney General. His activities would therefore abridge First Amendment rights which -- including academic freedom. Now in this case, --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- the Attorney General is performing --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) court in Maryland --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- exigent -- I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: A three judge court of Maryland has found this enough in it, hasn't it?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Oh, I --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: You mean, (Inaudible)

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: First, we believe that the three-judge court was in error and their examination of the issue was certainly a very limited one at the time.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But they accepted the oath which was construed by the Attorney General.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well, they did accept the oath as construed by the Attorney General but only because of the decision in Gerende. They felt incumbent upon them to accept it on the basis of Gerende. However, as we've suggested Gerende is dubious authority today.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Didn't you make the same argument in the three-judge court to making that?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: What? The Attorney General is without authority?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Oh, we can't say for sure that he's utterly without authority. What we're suggesting is that his basis for authority is this Court's decision in Gerende and the Court's -- and the Court of Appeals in Maryland's decision in Shub. Now both of those decisions presumably upheld an oath in specific terms.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And you say the Attorney General did not have the authority of the three-judge court as he did.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Justice Frankfurter in the Garner case --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: No, no, no.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: -- the same term as the Gerende.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: My question is --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: -- is it true that you say they did not have the authority?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's right. He does not have --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And the three-judge court said he did.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Is that correct?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That is in effect correct. But we would like to buttress this by suggesting in the Garner case, Justice Frankfurter suggested, and that was in the same term as Gerende. The Attorney General of Maryland did not give this assurance, the assurance he gave in the Gerende case. As a matter of personal relaxation, I believe a requirement. He was able to give it on the basis of the interpretation that the Court of Appeals of Maryland, the highest court of that state had placed upon the legislation. The Court of Appeals of Maryland has not pass upon this legislation since that statement was made. All he has to go on is the interpretation of the statute and the oath that was made in the Shub case back in 1950, and the operative legal rules with respect to First Amendment interest in the oath area, particularly with respect to vagueness, breadth, and indefiniteness, have been significantly changed. As he himself recognized by attempting to delete from the oath -- the phrase I -- and I am not a member of -- knowingly a member of an organization engaged in such an attempt. Now he can try to do that, he can delete it from the oath. But of course if we're right in our view that the oath can't be read without the statute, that won't get him anywhere. And we suggest further that it's entirely possible if he has no authority to act. So, in particularly in this First Amendment area, it's incumbent upon the Court to closely examine whether or not he has such authority to act.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Mr. Rosen, you've just referred to this, its (Inaudible) on the bench, as an oath.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: As I read it, it technically at least is a certification --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- rather than an oath. Is there any claim in this case that the petitioner was required to make an oath contrary to his religious scruples against taking an oath as contrasted with an affirmation?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: No. There was a claim that as a Quaker he had conscientious scruples against making this kind of a declaration but it wasn't necessarily the difference between an oath and affirmation or a certification.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: This is the equivalent of an affirmation, I see, I should (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes, I believe it is the equivalent of an affirmation but he has conscientious scruples against making this a --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: --affirmation.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Religious, conscientious scruples, they are constitutional or they -- or you think this violates his constitutional --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: He thinks that it's a viable --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- rights as a citizen.

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Because he'd -- can't understand what the oath means particularly when read with the statute. He thinks it may be in violation of his First Amendment free speech rights but also there are some questions --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I didn't see that in your brief and that's the (Inaudible).

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Well we didn't make much of a point of it --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Because I -- because its my understanding that some -- the disciples of certain religions --

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: That's right. We're not pressing this --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is fine but it violates their religious teachings to take any kind of an oath even though on the witness stand to tell the truth. Isn't that true, they'd rather affirm than take an oath?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes. But this -- we agree with the equivalent of an affirmation.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Affirmation?

turns.name: SANFORD JAY ROSEN
turns.text: Yes. Well, I see that my time is up Your Honors. Thank you very much.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Hawes.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. I should first like to distinguish this interpretation of Elfbrandt that was presented here with respect to the legislative gloss. Now in Elfbrandt, the reason that legislative gloss became important was that the perjury portion of the statute incorporated within it that the person who is taking the oath shall not knowingly and willfully become or remain a member of the Communist Party, etcetera. This is not the case in the Maryland certification situation because the Maryland perjury statute is simply a criminal statute for making a false statement. Now, the words “in one way or another,” we fail to see how these words could possibly give anyone any trouble. I mean, in any way. Its mere surplusage, its additional language was neither adds to nor takes away from the words or meaning of the oath itself. The Court on a number of occasions has found no constitutional difficulty from the vagueness standpoint in construing the words overthrow the government by force and violence. These words have been used in federal statutes such as the Smith Act and the vices of vagueness which has been found in other oaths and other laws and not been found in those words. The oath in Baggett versus Bullitt which was referable to a Washington statute which is identical to that in Maryland is distinguishable also. In that case, the oath said that, I certify that I'm not a subversive person as defined by the Act and there was no limitation upon the definition of subversive person in that case, in the oath that was taken in the State of Washington. And so the case of Baggett versus Bullitt does not constitute a precedent for overruling Gerende or making the Maryland oath as presently stated invalid.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Could you tell me where the form of oath that is prescribed in the statute?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Mr. Justice White, the form of oath is not prescribed in the statute.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible) What about the --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: I believe it was. I'm not certain of that.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Does the latter says the -- in light of the (Inaudible) the Maryland law has been modified by emanating words so and so forth. (Inaudible) -- of the modified loyalty oath decided by the court. There's no -- where is the authority in the statute (Inaudible)? Is there any mention of an oath from the statute or in virtue of (Inaudible)?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes there is.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Do you see any mention of an oath (Inaudible)?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well 11 contains the authorization for a state agency to make --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Rules and regulations --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: - rules and regulations --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: -- and procedures.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Now, who makes the oath?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: The -- they can set up a procedure and the procedure involved here as I see it would be a written statement intending --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, that's the last --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Containing this is 11 said.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Containing a notice that it is subject to the penalties of perjury.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well I know what it says -- what's this mean, in securing any facts necessary, the applicant shall be required to sign a written statement. Is that what we're talking about? Containing answers to such inquiries is maybe immaterial.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: So who -- then who promulgates the form of the employee has to sign whether you -- suppose an oath or a certification or what it is. Actually, it's just a -- according to the statute it's just a written statement isn't it, containing answers to inquiry?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That would be true under --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: As the Attorney General always drafted the form of oaths used in this?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well he did in this case. And -- but whether he did in the initial case, I can't answer it right now because I don't know.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: And is it your position that he has the authority to do that under this Section?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: No, he would have the authority to do that under his general authority as Attorney General to -- as a legal adviser to the various agencies of state government. And this would be a function of his rendering legal advice to write or create a -- an oath which would pass the constitutional standards and would also conform with the intent of the Act.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, at least there's no statutory requirement that the broader -- that is being modified here by the Attorney General because the form was not specified by the statute.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's right, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Are there any written regulations that you know of, of any department in the state of Maryland with respect to this matter?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: With respect to University of Maryland, it's not by written regulation. It's by form which was adopted --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But it's a --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: -- form of the oath.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Is there any regulation by any other department that you know of?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: I'd -- possibly in Baltimore City, the school board has affirmed. This isn't part of this case -- it is not in the record and I couldn't say for certain.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr.(Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Since there are -- pardon me. Does this certification -- does it apply to the University of Maryland, the school people?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Applies to every employee in the State of Maryland?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: It applies to all employees except the laborers.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Except laborers.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: All, except labors.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Laborers.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Oh, laborers. Working for the state, is that right?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Mr. Hawes, supposed an employee was a member of the Communist Party of the United States, but he was a completely passive, nominal -- their having been a determination that the Communist Party of United States, is an organization engaged to overthrow by force or violence. But he safely say, “I am not engaged in any one way -- in one way or another in an attempt to overthrow and so forth the (Inaudible)?”

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: I would think it would be -- the question there as to whether his involvement in the Communist Party was more than just as you stated.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, I'm giving you the -- I'm giving you the basis. The hypothetical is he's a purely nominal passive member of an organization which is engaged.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That would not be enough under this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Would he be sure of that?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: I think so because this Court said so.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Oh, he's engaged in one way or another in the event.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well, this Court --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: -- has said that this is not in the Elfbrandt case.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: And we've stricken out that clause to be a -- an oath that might have covered situation and --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: We struck out the evidence --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: You struck out the knowing membership provision.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But you're confident that he could sign this without this conformity?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: With that clause stricken, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, as I understand the argument of your opponent, perhaps it runs this way that this statement is made pursuant to Section 13 of the subversiveness, Subversive Activities Act of 1949, is that right?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: The particular oath involved in this case does say that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: It's made pursuant to that section of the law.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes. No, the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Is that --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: -- the form has on there -- apparently what happened was that --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: -- when it went to the printer, the printer left on that at a reference to Section 13.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, whoever left it on, its there.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: In this particular case, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And the -- there you are in Section 13, now your opponent asks -- so says that the -- if Mr. Whitehill is charged with knowledge of what's in Section 13. And that Section 13 says that the Mr. Whitehill and many state employees has to make a written statement which shall contain notice subject to the penalties of perjury, that he is not a subversive person as defined in this Article and then it goes on and that he is not a member of a subversive organization or a foreign subversive organization that is more fully defined in this Article. Now I suppose if the argument of your opponent is that no matter what this statement says that since it's made, since the statement is made pursuant to Section 13 of the Act with which Professor Whitehill noticed -- what Professor Whitehill is charged, that Professor Whitehill is saying that he is not a subversive person as defined in the Article and not a member of a subversive organization or a foreign subversive organization as defined in the Act. Our key to that I assume on your -- Attorney General has (Inaudible) in the -- has effectively rewritten Section 13. Isn't that right -- isn't that what (Inaudible)?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: I would answer that question in two ways. One, we could say very recently that Section 13 has been rewritten and modified and (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Could you speak a little louder please?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes sir. That Section 13 has been modified by the oath prescribed by the Attorney General which was presented to this individual. Now, this gets into the question of severability and so forth which I will reach later.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, would you concede --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: But secondly --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: Do you -- but you concede that if -- that Section 13 was at least in part invalidated by Baggett against Bullitt?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: And that if the oath really did require the professor to -- this term, what Section 13 requires in this term that'd be unconstitutional under Bagget against Bullit.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: If all the words of Section 13 were in the oath, that is correct. But, secondly I would say this, that if this reference to Section 13 becomes a basis for this Court's decision that this would involve only this one individual and it would not preclude Maryland's requiring him to execute a certification which did not have a reference to Section 13 as a precondition to (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I know, but I don't -- we have before us is this case. That's the one presumably that the (Inaudible).

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And that does have the reference to Section 13.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's correct, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Or whether or not the reference to Section 13 is on a piece of paper that he is required to sign, is there any other authority in Maryland's state law except 13 for anybody to require any certification like this one.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Oh, we say that there's authority under Section 11 and there's one under --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Where do we get the facts in -- for 11?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Under 15.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: On page 10A of the petitioner's brief or the appellant's brief, I think (Inaudible)?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes, it is in the appendix, Section 10A. Now, in Section 15, there is a requirement for an oath of candidates for office. That's not the question -- the oath involved here. And Section 13 only involves those persons who are in employment on June 1, 1949 and that's not the case here because this man is an applicant and not an employee.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Of course the statement under Section 11 is only an aid of the termination whether that he was a subversive person.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's correct, but a subversive person --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, how we -- only further along under 11 on the (Inaudible).

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Because, first --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That's -- that the only authority for the statement at all is in aid of a determination whether he's a subversive person, is that right? That's the only authority you have to ask any certificate.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's right, for an applicant.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Hawes, is that -- this reference in Section 13 is still on defense?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Then he's taken the oath.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: At that time it was brought up in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: When was that?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: It would have been at the time of the argument. I believe it was February last year.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You may find it (Inaudible). Continue your argument.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Thank you Mr. Chief Justice. The question of the authority of the Attorney General is apparently still bothering the Court. And I should once again try to establish where this authority comes from and how it affects this interpretation of the law here. Now, this may not be in the brief, but in Article 5, Section 3 of the Maryland constitution, it is stated that the Attorney General shall give his opinion in writing on any legal matter or subject. And in Code Article 32A of the Maryland Annotated Code Section 3, the Attorney General shall have general charge supervision and direction of a legal business of the other state and shall be legal adviser and representative of and perform all legal work for boards, institutions, departments, etcetera of the state. Now the University of Maryland of course is a department, an institution or an agency of the state. The applicable part of Section 11 of the overact is that every board in this case would be the Board of Regents or other agency of the state which employs --and so forth employee shall establish by rules, regulations or otherwise procedures designed to ascertain before any person is appointed or employed that he or she as the case maybe is not a subversive person and that there are no reasonable grounds to believe that such persons are subversive persons. Now, this is quite similar to the charge in Section 15 which was construed by this Court in Gerende where an affidavit was required for a person who is a candidate for election. That he is -- he or she is not a subversive person as defined in this Article. And our argument here is that if the oath prescribed here which was the one that this Court said was sufficient under Gerende can be given under Section 15 that certainly the same oath should be able to be given under Section 11 of the Act.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Where is the direct power of the Attorney General to make rules and regulations that are binding upon any officer of the state?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well, the --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Or employee?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: The oath here is not prescribed in that manner Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: No, but that --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: The --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Do you contend that the -- he has a right to make rules or regulations for the department (Voice Overlap).

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But how does it -- that power, how is that power transferred to this situation?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well, in the letter that is in the appendix to appellant's brief on page 18A, which is a second page to the letter from Attorney General Finan to Governor Tawes dated May 13, 1966. In that letter, the Attorney General suggested -- and this is giving his legal advice to the agency -- to all of state agencies. In this case, to the governor, to the Secretary of State, commissioner-personnel, and a copy of course went to the University of Maryland. And on the basis of this letter, it is up to the state agency involved to prescribe the oath and to give it to its employees. Now, there is one distinction here with respect to commissioner-personnel -- he does not prescribe the personnel procedures for the University of Maryland because of the Autonomy Act of 1952. And this clause --

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: So the university took the Attorney General's advice I gather.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: As to the body of the oath, it did.

turns.name: JUSTTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Except -- they made it apparently an error in having it printed with the reference to Section 13 which shouldn't have been in there. And this is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But I take it that the advice of the Attorney General was never binding in any agencies. It's only a guide, isn't it?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: It --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But what I have reference that apparently it's a decision of your Court of Appeals, the (Inaudible) case, you're familiar with I'm sure where it says the opinions of the Attorney General are not binding, but are entitled to careful consideration of service guides to those charge of the administration of law. Is that the rule in Maryland?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That is correct because only a court can bind the department to a particular interpretation of law. However, as a practical matter, the department do follow the Attorneys General -- Attorney General's advice.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I understood counsel to say that they were giving this oath in various forms throughout the state today.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: This is not in the record and I have no personal knowledge of this. If it's true, it's something that's outside of the record.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now in this letter that he wrote -- that the Attorney General wrote to the governor he said, “In light of the -- he grant decisions. The Maryland loyalty pledge has been modified by eliminating the words "and I am not knowingly a member of an organization engaged in such an attempt" to read as follows and then he quotes the present oath. Now who modified it?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well, the Attorney General did here.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: The Attorney General modified it?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, does he -- where did he have -- where do you get the power to modify any law of the State of Maryland?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well, the oath isn't prescribed in that form by law. So he is modifying a procedure which a department is using under Section 11 of the Act.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Who made that procedure? Who established the original procedure?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: I -- probably commissioner-personnel or the -- for the regions in the case of the University of Maryland on the advice of the Attorney General. They actually do the printing of -- at the University with respect to their oaths.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well it -- it couldn't be the Secretary of State or commissioner-personnel because you say the offices advised him. This office is advising the Secretary of State and the commissioner-personnel of the modified loyalty pledge devised by this office to meet the constitutional standards requiring a decision there is on the case, so evidently they didn't do it, did they?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes, they did.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well they might have done it following the modification by the Attorney General.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Oh, in other words (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) where is the power of the Attorney General to modify an Act of the legislature which says one thing? Or do you contend that you have broad general powers to make regulations of this kind of change and it will.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well, the -- let's look at this way. The -- we're talking about two O's here. First, the one prior to 1966 which is in identical words of what this Court said in Gerende, the Attorney General could advise the state agencies as conforming with I guess the Maryland law and satisfying its requirements completely. Now, what the Attorney General has done here is to limit that further. Now if the department had not taken his advice and used the old pledge, we might be up here on a different case. But in this case, he did not, the department -- of the Board of Regents took the Attorney General's advice and use this to take the form. Now with respect to the negative nature of the oath which was brought up, we would say this is -- in the very fact that you could have an oath, this doesn't differ too much from the certifications that the facts are true and correct and so forth that one makes on your income tax forms, employment applications, auto registration, auto licenses and so forth. And on those -- on certain types of applications, an applicant maybe asked whether he is taking narcotics or his -- a consumer of a certain amount of alcohol or certain other very personnel type of questions which don't reach First Amendment freedoms, beliefs or speech and so forth but nevertheless or in the same type of form that this particular certification is. Now, with respect to the -- the extent of the oath in reaching the innocent person who is a mere member of an organization or something of this sort, it should be noted that the oath itself -- the title to the other law itself -- the Act itself states that this is an Act to add a new Article making it a crime to commit acts or advocate acts intended to affect the overthrow of the government and so forth by an -- violence and so forth or to attempt or conspire to do so. Now, this Title and the Act itself which construed by the Maryland Court as set forth in our brief, as its intention is to prevent infiltration into our state, county and municipal governments of person who are engaged in one way or another in the attempt to overthrow the government by force or violence. And this interpretation of the intent of the Act was taken in by this Court in Gerende to be the full meaning of it. And it is our contention that a certification in this language therefore is sufficient under all the cases involving loyalty oaths to this time. And that this Court would have to overrule Gerende and possibly Garner or Cramp and at least distinguish these cases if it were to make an adverse ruling with respect to this oath on the grounds of vagueness.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Hawes, just one more thing if you were -- have you finished -- had you finished?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I just want to ask you one more thing. I understood Professor Rosen to say that the -- your Court of Appeals had never passed upon this particular form of the declaration, is that right?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes, it passed upon a form which was larger than this one.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. But has never passed on this interpretation of the Attorney General?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Eliminating the --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: -- words in 1966.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, is your Court of Appeals bound to follow the interpretation of the Attorney General?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: It is not bound to follow it but it would certain (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So what you've -- what you have been arguing to us is premised upon the interpretation of the Attorney General and not upon the interpretation of your Court.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: No, because the portion of the oath which is now being used was approved by the Court. There were just some additional words which were thrown out in 1966, which may or may not be valid or invalid that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: In which may or may not be valid, wouldn't that be up to this Court to determine?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: The Maryland Court?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: If they were still being used. But if it isn't being used, it doesn't seem to make any difference.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, it might not be up to the Court of Appeals of Maryland ultimately rather than your State Attorney General to decide whether or not this certification is sufficient in compliance with the Maryland statute.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Well, if --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That's never been before the Court, has it, the Maryland Court of Appeals?

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's correct, it has not.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The -- your Attorney General has advised that it is sufficient in compliance but as to what you've just told --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- the Chief Justice that there's no way binding upon the Maryland Court of Appeals.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: What the -- this Court can be assured that the Maryland Court will find the law to be constitutional.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, that's a separate question.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The question that I thought -- the Chief Justice's question is directed to in any event require -- what my question is directed to is whether or not this certification is sufficient in compliance with the Maryland statute. And that's never been --

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- passed upon by the Court of Appeals of Maryland.

turns.name: LORING E. HAWES
turns.text: That's correct.
